10.1016/j.jmp.2018.08.006
Data are processed, analyzed and interpreted to reveal facts and mechanisms that can help to understand outcomes and to predict the future. Data collection and analysis never end, in part because things change; yesterday’s data are not always a good indication for tomorrow. Current psychological debates include concerns of this nature. First, there is the recent burgeoning literature on the so-called “replication crisis” (e.g.,  Maxwell, Lau, & Howard, 2015 ). To what extent are experiments replicable, even under carefully controlled conditions with samples taken from identical or very similar populations? Second, there is the classic “external validity” problem in new guise, namely that the bulk of experimental psychological evidence is limited to samples from North American undergraduate university students. Will theories and hypotheses in cognitive, social, and/or clinical psychology hold up when they are tested on samples from other populations  (Henrich, Heine, & Norenzayan, 2010) ? Furthermore, how reliable are data from one population when used to make predictions or assessments of a population whose degree of difference is uncertain?

Contemporary statistical methods necessarily fall silent on these questions unless strong assumptions are made about the future or alternative populations. For instance, simulating data from a Bayesian posterior predictive distribution addresses the question of replicability only if we assume that the next study will be conducted on a population in an environment exactly like those obtained in the initial study. If we are not willing to make such a strong assumption, then we must regard the models that we build as always uncertain, sometimes greatly so, because the world is in flux.

Data-based prediction in a changing world requires two complementary capabilities: versatile modeling, integrated with the management of uncertainty. This paper develops a response to this challenge. To articulate and illustrate our approach, we focus on statistical models of bounded random variables, associated with additional uncertainties that are non-probabilistic. Doubly-bounded random variables occur throughout many disciplines, including psychology. The most common examples are proportions, percentages, and rates, such as probability judgments, the proportion of the brain’s volume occupied by a specific part of the brain, and market share percentages. Many widely used psychometric scales also are doubly-bounded.

We employ CDF-quantile distributions  (Smithson & Shou, 2017)  to model the probabilistic aspects of these phenomena, partly because of their tractability and flexibility. Non-probabilistic uncertainties associated with these processes arise from the limitations of our understanding, and even more from the endless potential for unanticipated innovation, invention and discovery that accompany all human affairs, as described by  Popper  ( 1982 , pp. 80–81, 109) and by  Shackle  ( 1972 , pp. 3–4, 156, 239, 401–402). (See also  Ben-Haim, 2007 ). These are Knightian uncertainties  (Knight, 1921)  and we use info-gap decision theory to model and manage them ( Ben-Haim, 2006 ; info-gap.com).

Sections  2 and 3  provide introductions to info-gap decision theory and to CDF-Quantile distributions, respectively. Section  4  summarizes basic mathematical properties of CDF-Quantile distributions that will be used subsequently. Sections  5 and 6  are the main parts of the paper. Section  5  discusses prediction of the quantile rank for a population in which the CDF-Quantile shape and location parameters are uncertain. This illustrates the combination of info-gap analysis of robustness to non-probabilistic uncertainty in parameters, with CDF-Quantile modeling of the data. Section  6  performs a similar analysis in which the uncertainty is in the entire shape of the distributions, not only in parameter values. In both examples the emphasis is on making reliable probabilistic assessments despite the non-probabilistic uncertainty, where reliability is defined as robustness to non-probabilistic uncertainty. We show that the putatively optimal assessment, i.e., the one that best fits the data, is not necessarily the most robust to uncertainty.

Info-gap theory is a method for prioritizing options and making choices and decisions under severe uncertainty  (Ben-Haim, 2006, 2010) . The options might be operational alternatives (design a therapy, choose a budget, decide to intervene or not, etc.) or more abstract decisions (choose a model structure, make a forecast, formulate a policy, etc.). Decisions are based on data, scientific theories, empirical relations, knowledge and contextual understanding, all of which we will refer to as one’s  models , and these models often recognize and quantify uncertainty.

Info-gap theory has been applied to decision problems in many fields, including various areas of engineering  (Chinnappen-Rimer & Hancke, 2011; Harp & Vesselinov, 2013; Kanno & Takewaki, 2006)  biological conservation  (Burgman, 2005)  economics  (Ben-Haim, 2010; Knoke, 2008)  medicine  (Ben-Haim, Zetola, & Dacso, 2012)  national security  (Moffitt, Stranlund, & Field, 2005)  public policy  (Hall et al., 2012)  and more (info-gap.com). Info-gap robust-satisficing has been discussed non-technically elsewhere  (Ben-Haim, 2012a, 2012b, 2018; Schwartz, Ben-Haim, & Dacso, 2011) .

Uncertainty is often modeled with probability distributions. If the probability distributions are correct and comprehensive then one can exploit the models exhaustively to reliably achieve stochastically optimal outcomes, and one does not need info-gap theory. However, if one’s models will be much better next year when new knowledge has become available (but you must decide now), or if processes are changing in poorly known ways, or if important factors will be determined beyond your knowledge or control, then one faces severe uncertainties and info-gap theory might help. This section presents an intuitive discussion of two basic ideas of info-gap theory: satisficing and robustness. More systematic discussion appears later.

Knight (1921)  distinguished between what he called “risk” (for which probability distributions are known) and “true uncertainty” (for which probability distributions are not known). Knightian (“true”) uncertainty reflects ignorance of many things, including underlying processes, functional relationships, strategies or intentions of relevant actors, future events, inventions, discoveries, surprises and so on. Info-gap models of uncertainty provide a non-probabilistic quantification of Knightian uncertainty. An info-gap is the disparity between what you  do know  (or think to be true) and what you  need to know  for making a reliable or responsible decision (the necessary knowledge may be uncertain). An info-gap is not ignorance per se, but rather those aspects of one’s Knightian uncertainty that bear on a pending decision and the quality of its outcome.

An info-gap model of uncertainty is particularly suitable for representing uncertainty in the shape of a function. For instance, one might have an estimate of the dose–response curve for a new drug, or of the severity of emotional disturbance as a function of a test score, or of a probability density function (pdf), but the shape of the function (e.g. the shape of the dose–response curve, or the shape of the test-score function, or the tails of the pdf) may be highly uncertain. Info-gap models are also widely used to represent uncertainty in parameters or vectors or sometimes uncertainty in sets of such entities.

Decision makers often try to optimize the outcome of their decisions. That is usually approached by using one’s best models to predict the outcomes of the various options, and then choosing the option whose predicted outcome is best. The aspiration for excellence is commendable, but outcome-optimization may be costly, or one may not really need the best possible outcome.  Schwartz (2004)  discusses the irrelevance of optimal outcomes in many situations.

Outcome-optimization –using one’s models to choose the decision whose predicted outcome is best –works fine when the models are pretty good, because exhaustively exploiting good models will usually lead to good outcomes.

However, when one faces major info-gaps, one’s models contain major errors or lacunae, and exhaustively exploiting the models can be unrealistic, unreliable, and can lead to undesired outcomes  (Ben-Haim, 2012a) . Under severe uncertainty it is better to ask: what outcomes are critical and must be achieved? This is the idea of  satisficing  introduced by Herbert  Simon (1956) : achieving a satisfactory or acceptable, but not necessarily optimal, outcome —‘good enough’ according to an explicitly stated set of criteria. While it is true that the goal of research is to provide reliable, accurate, and precise description, it is also true that putatively optimal descriptions can err fundamentally due to Knightian uncertainty. A central contribution of this paper is to establish a method for managing this challenge.

Planners, designers and decision makers in all fields have used the language of optimization (the lightest, the strongest, the fastest) for ages. In practice, however, satisficing is very widespread though not always recognized as such. Engineers satisfy design specifications (light enough, strong enough, fast enough). Stock brokers, business people, and investors of all sorts do not really need to maximize profits; they only need to beat the competition, or improve on last year, or meet the customer’s demands. Beating the competition means satisficing a goal. The same can be said of the clinical psychologist who must prevent recidivism while not realistically hoping for total social re-adjustment, or the foreign aid planner who must raise public health to international standards.

Once the decision maker identifies the critical goals or outcomes that must be achieved, the next step is to decide about something or choose an action that will achieve those goals despite current ignorance or unknown future surprises. A decision has high  robustness  if it satisfices the performance requirements over a wide range of unanticipated contingencies. Conversely, a decision has low robustness if even small errors in our knowledge can prevent achievement of the critical goals. The  robust-satisficing  decision maker prioritizes the alternatives in terms of their robustness against uncertainty for achieving the critical goals.

We now define info-gap models of uncertainty and in Section  2.3  we define info-gap robustness and discuss two theorems. In Section  2.4  we discuss a toy example. These ideas have been extensively discussed before so we will be concise  (Ben-Haim, 1999, 2000a, 2000b, 2001, 2006, 2010) .

The info-gap robustness function depends on 3 entities: a system model, an info-gap model of uncertainty, and a performance requirement. (Multiple performance requirements can also be imposed, but we do not encounter this in the present paper.) The system model is a real-valued function,

                               d

                               f

                            d

                            f 
                         . An info-gap model,  
                            U 
                            
                               ( 
                               h 
                               ) 
                            
                          for  
                            h 
                            ≥ 
                            0

                            f 
                         . The performance requirement is that the outcome must not exceed a critical value,  
                            ε

                            R 
                            
                               ( 
                               d 
                               , 
                               f 
                               ) 
                            
                            ≤ 
                            ε 
                         . (The performance requirement may be that the outcome must be no less than a critical value. The generic properties discussed below hold in this case as well.)

The following theorem expresses a property of all info-gap robustness functions: the robustness decreases as the performance requirement becomes more demanding.

                                                 ε 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                           , 
                                           d 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        > 
                                        
                                           
                                              h 
                                           
                                           
                                              ̂ 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 ε 
                                              
                                              
                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                           , 
                                           d 
                                           )

This proposition states that the robustness function is strictly monotonic on the set of performance requirements that can be satisfied. That is, robustness trades off against performance. More precisely, requirement  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          is more demanding that requirement  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          (because  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            < 
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         ), and the robustness (to uncertainty) for satisfying  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          is strictly less than the robustness for satisfying  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         . More colloquially, this proposition is sometimes called the Pessimist’s Theorem because any healthy pessimist knows that more demanding requirements are more difficult to achieve. This theorem appears in  Ben-Haim (2000a)  as theorem 1, including a proof.

The following definition is necessary for the next proposition.

The proofs of this and all subsequent propositions appear in  Appendix A .

This proposition asserts that the robustness for achieving the putative outcome,  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  ˜ 
                               
                            
                         , is zero.  
                            
                               
                                  ε 
                               
                               
                                  ˜ 
                               
                            
                          is the predicted outcome of decision  
                            d 
                          based, presumably, on the best available information and understanding. In other words, predicted outcomes are an unreliable basis for evaluating the performance of decision alternatives. Instead, performance evaluation should be based on the robustness for achieving acceptable outcomes. This will become particularly pertinent when we encounter decision alternatives whose robustness curves cross one another.

People have a trait whose value is either ‘Y’ or ‘N’. An individual has  
                            M 
                          attributes whose values are real numbers:  
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                            … 
                            , 
                            
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  M 
                               
                            
                         . The attributes are combined to assess the trait as a weighted sum,  
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  M 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         , where the weights  
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          are non-negative and sum up to one. The analyst will choose a threshold value,  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                         , and declare that the person’s trait is ‘N’ if and only if the weighted attribute exceeds a threshold:  
                            
                               (9) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        M 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        w 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        y 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≥ 
                                  
                                     
                                        z 
                                     
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          is a decision variable controlled by the analyst. The choice of this threshold,  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                         , may depend on prior experience, or may be derived from the analysis of uncertainty in the measurements. In this preliminary example, however, we focus not on the choice of  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                         , but rather on the possible exclusion of measurements with large errors.

                                        y 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˜

                         The horizon of uncertainty,  
                            h 
                         , is dimensionless.

The question we ask is: are we justified in excluding some of the measurements (e.g. those with large estimated error), and if so, which ones and why? We use the robustness function to develop an answer.

                         This implies that removal of this measurement strictly decreases the robustness, which would never be desired. That measurement should be retained.

The robustness curve labeled ‘2’ intersects

                         Eq.  (18)  implies that

                                  ˜ 
                               
                            
                         , have no robustness (this is the zeroing property). We must exploit the trade off property to raise the robustness by relaxing the threshold. The slope of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        h 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̂

                                        ˜ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                         : the same rise in robustness is obtained with a lower reduction in threshold for  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        h 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̂ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          than for  
                            
                               
                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         . Consequently the robustness curves cross one another, entailing the possibility of a reversal of preference between these two options. Specifically,  
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  ˜ 
                               
                            
                          is more robust (and hence preferred) at large  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                         , while  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        y 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˜ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          is more robust (and hence preferred) at lower  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                         . The choice between these alternatives depends on the value of  
                            
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          required, in light of the corresponding value of robustness.

We introduce here the recently developed “CDF-Quantile” family of two-parameter distributions with support (0,1), first briefly described in  Verkuilen and Smithson (2012)  and  Smithson and Merkle (2014) , elaborated by  Smithson and Shou (2017)  and implemented in an R package and SAS macro by  Shou and Smithson (2017) . Maximum likelihood estimation therefore is feasible for members of this family, and is the estimation technique adopted by  Shou and Smithson (2017)  in the  cdfquantreg  package, which implements regression modeling capabilities for 36 CDF-Quantile distributions. Additional relevant properties shared by the members of the family include the following (proofs and details are provided in  Smithson and Shou (2017) ):

1. 
                            The pdfs  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     μ 
                                     , 
                                     σ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                are self-dual in this respect:  
                                  g

                                        , 
                                        μ 
                                        , 
                                        σ

                                  g

                                        −

                                        , 
                                        σ 
                                     
                                  
                               .

                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     )

                                  x 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                if  
                                  μ 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                               . When  
                                  H 
                                  = 
                                  F 
                                then  
                                  G 
                                is the uniform distribution if  
                                  μ 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                                and  
                                  σ 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               .

                            The median is a function solely of the location parameter  
                                  μ 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            4. 
                            The scale parameter  
                                  σ 
                                is a dispersion parameter controlling the spread of other quantiles around the median.

                            The likelihood function is explicit, as are the gradient and Hessian.

                            The CDF-Quantile family therefore is capable of fitting distribution shapes that alternatives, such as the beta distribution, cannot.

The pdfs  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     μ 
                                     , 
                                     σ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                are self-dual in this respect:  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        μ 
                                        , 
                                        σ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  g 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                        − 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        − 
                                        μ 
                                        , 
                                        σ 
                                     
                                  
                               .

G 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is symmetrical at  
                                  x 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                if  
                                  μ 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                               . When  
                                  H 
                                  = 
                                  F 
                                then  
                                  G 
                                is the uniform distribution if  
                                  μ 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                                and  
                                  σ 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               .

The median is a function solely of the location parameter  
                                  μ 
                               .

The scale parameter  
                                  σ 
                                is a dispersion parameter controlling the spread of other quantiles around the median.

The likelihood function is explicit, as are the gradient and Hessian.

The 36 distributions comprise four subfamilies, distinguished by the distribution tail-behavior.

The CDF-Quantile family therefore is capable of fitting distribution shapes that alternatives, such as the beta distribution, cannot.

Consider a CDF-quantile distribution, defined by  Smithson and Shou (2017)  as the following cumulative distribution function (CDF):  
                         
                            (27) 
                            
                               G 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               F 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 H 
                                              
                                              
                                                 − 
                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              x 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           − 
                                           μ 
                                        
                                        
                                           σ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      where  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         H 
                         
                            ( 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       are cumulative probability distribution functions defined on the entire real line and whose densities are symmetric around the origin. We will assume that  
                         G 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            , 
                            σ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is normalized on the domain of  
                         x 
                      , meaning that  
                         G 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            , 
                            σ 
                            ) 
                         
                       varies from 0 to 1 as  
                         x 
                       varies over its domain.

G 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            , 
                            σ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is undefined if  
                         σ 
                         = 
                         0 
                      . However, because  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                       is defined on the entire real line, we can assert:  
                         
                            (28) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     lim 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     → 
                                     ∞ 
                                  
                               
                               F 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                         
                      This limit arises as  
                         σ 
                       approaches zero, which will occur occasionally in the subsequent development. We will abbreviate this asymptotic assertion by writing, or implying,  
                         F 
                         
                            ( 
                            ∞ 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         1 
                      .

We now define notation that will be used extensively later. Let  
                         
                            
                               μ 
                            
                            
                               ̂ 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               σ

                         μ 
                       and

                         γ 
                       in  
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ]

                               x 
                            
                            
                               γ 
                            
                         
                       denote the corresponding quantile for  
                         G 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      . That is:  
                         
                            (29) 
                            
                               G 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        γ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ,

                                        ̂

                                        ̂ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               γ

                            increases.

                               G

                                  x

                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            in Eq.   (27)

                            increases.

Proposition 4 
                         
                            The distribution function  
                            
                               G 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  )

                            increases. Specifically: 
                         
                         
                            
                               • 
                               
                            
                            
                               G 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x

                                     H 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1

                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̂ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is non-negative.

                               G 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            does not decrease as  
                            
                               σ

                                     H 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̂ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is negative.

We are concerned in this section, and in Section  6 , with predicting quantile ranks subject to uncertainty in underlying probabilistic entities. A related problem is to predict the quantiles themselves. This is explored in  Appendix B .  Propositions 12–14  in  Appendix B  are directly analogous to  Propositions 5–7 , respectively, to be discussed here.  Proposition 15  establishes a relation between the robustness for predicting quantile ranks, Eq.  (34)  in Section  5.1 , and the robustness for predicting quantiles that is developed in  Appendix B .

Section  5.1  formulates the info-gap robustness function and establishes properties that are used subsequently in decision and assessment. Section  5.2  extends the discussion to the 36 explicit families of CDF-Quantile distributions. Sections  5.3 and 5.4  present examples illustrating the practical implications of the theoretical results. Throughout this section we consider info-gap uncertainty in parameters, and in Section  6  we consider info-gap uncertainty in the shape of the probability distributions.

Suppose that a random sample of data from a well-defined population is available from which estimates of the location parameter,  
                            μ 
                         , and the shape parameter,  
                            σ 
                         , can be obtained (e.g., via maximum-likelihood estimation). Sampling error is the relevant uncertainty in making inferences about the population from which the sample came. However, additional uncertainties regarding  
                            μ 
                          and  
                            σ 
                          arise if we wish to make inferences from the sample estimates to other populations, or even the same population at a different time. The estimated location parameter is denoted  
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         , with an estimated error  
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                          whose meaning is that  
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          may err as much as  
                            ± 
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                          or more (typically these would be a sample estimate,  
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         , and the standard error of that estimate,  
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                         ). We note that  
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                          is positive. The shape parameter is usually estimated by estimating its logarithm,  
                            ln 
                            σ 
                         . Let  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂

                            σ

                            ln 
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂

                            σ 
                         . Let  
                            
                               
                                  w 
                               
                               
                                  σ

                            ln 
                            σ

                                  σ

                            ln

                            ±

                            h

                            h

Note that the uncertain fractional error on  
                            ln 
                            σ

                            σ

                            h

                            h 
                         , is non-negative and unbounded. Greater  
                            h 
                          implies greater uncertainty. However, there is no probabilistic content to the value of  
                            h 
                          because it reflects Knightian uncertainty. Thus, the family of intervals around  
                            σ 
                          cannot be interpreted probabilistically. Stated differently, if one did have a probability distribution for  
                            h 
                         , then one could interpret these intervals probabilistically. However, we face non-probabilistic Knightian uncertainty so we cannot interpret them probabilistically.

                                  r

                         . In practical terms, this means that knowledge of one function entirely determines the other function, because they are both monotonic. In particular, a plot of  
                            h 
                          (vertically) vs.  
                            m 
                            
                               ( 
                               h 
                               , 
                               γ 
                               , 
                               q 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          (horizontally) is identical to a plot of  
                            
                               
                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               ε 
                               , 
                               γ 
                               , 
                               q 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          (vertically) vs.  
                            ε 
                          (horizontally).

                            ε

Our first example focuses on the choice among alternative CDF-Quantile models, based on goodness-of-fit to data and robustness. We hasten to point out that these certainly are not the only (and sometimes not the best) criteria for selecting a statistical model. Where possible, such a choice should be determined largely by appropriate theoretical considerations. For example, given a particular data-generating mechanism one might argue that the beta distribution is the appropriate one to use. That said, as  Kieschnick and McCullough (2003, p. 95)  observe, regarding proportions “there is no clear agreement on the data generating process for such data. Thus, we fit the different regression models to a common data set using a common specification of the regressors to determine which regression model best describes the data”. In any case, the primary purpose of our examples in this paper is to illustrate the trade-off between goodness of fit and robustness. We therefore compare statistical models that differ on the one and ask how they differ on the other.

We first present the results from fitting candidate CFD-Quantile distributions to a sample of data, identifying the best-fitting models based on these distributions. We then investigate the models’ robustness under uncertainty about their parameters with the performance requirement on the quantile rank error. A crucial question is whether the best-fitting models also are the most robust.

The fourth Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) report uses verbal phrases such as “likely” and “unlikely” to describe the uncertainties in climate science, and their report includes a set of guidelines for translating such phrases into numerical ranges (e.g., “very likely” is intended to have a range from 0.9 to 1).  Budescu, Broomell, and Por (2009)  conducted an experimental study of lay interpretations of these phrases, using 13 sentences from the IPCC report. The researchers asked 223 participants to provide numerical estimates of the probabilities to which they believed each sentence referred. For example, participants were presented with the sentence “The Greenland ice sheet and other Arctic ice fields likely contributed no more than 4 m of the observed sea level rise”. They then were asked what probability they thought the report authors may have had in mind for the term “likely” in this sentence. Participants were required to provide their lowest, highest, and their best numerical estimates of this probability.

Budescu et al. (2009)  found that participants’ “best” estimates were more regressive (toward the middle of the [0, 1] interval) than the IPCC stipulations.  Smithson, Budescu, Broomell, and Por (2012)  reanalyzed these data using beta regression and reported that the negatively-worded probability expressions (e.g., “unlikely”) produced more regressive means and larger standard deviations than their positively-worded counterparts.  Smithson and Shou (2017)  applied CDF-Quantile distributions to modeling these data and, although their models confirmed the finding of greater dispersion for negatively-worded expressions, they found that positively-worded probability expressions produced more regressive medians.

The models reported here are from  Smithson and Shou (2017) . They focus on six of the 13 sentences, namely those containing the probability expressions “likely” and “unlikely”. They also limit analysis to the “best” estimates given by the 223 subjects in their interpretations (thereby omitting their lower and upper estimates). Their models ignore dependence ( Shou and Smithson (2017)  present a multivariate model of these data that models the dependency structure via a copula with CDF-Quantile marginal distributions).

Table 1  displays two distributions with the best log-likelihoods for the models fitting them to the IPCC data in the  Smithson and Shou (2017)  example, plus one (the logit-logistic) whose fit is not as good. The “condit”. column lists the two types of probability phrases that participants were asked to interpret. The “neg.” (negative-wording) condition includes the three sentences using “unlikely”, while the “pos.” (positive-wording) condition includes the three “likely” sentences. Each distribution is fitted to the neg. and pos. data combined.

The three distributions may be defined as follows. The T2–T2 distribution has a

Are the best-fitting distributions also the most robust under Knightian uncertainty? If not, how should such an outcome inform a decision-maker’s preferences, when choosing a model to make predictions about other populations? We investigate these questions for the performance requirement on the quantile rank error. These findings could justifiably give an analyst pause before dismissing the Logit-Logistic model in favor of the T2–T2 or ArcSinh-Cauchy solely on grounds of their better fit, as we now explain.

The T2–T2 and arcsinh-Cauchy distributions do fit the data better than Logit-Logistic distribution. However, we wish to predict the quantile rank in a situation that differs –to an unknown degree –from that in which the data were obtained. We know the estimated values of  
                            μ 
                          and  
                            σ 
                          in the measured situation, but we are uncertain how different they are in the un-measured situation. Suppose that we require that the predicted quantile rank err by no more than 0.07. We have substantially greater confidence that the Logit-Logistic prediction satisfies this requirement than either the T2–T2 or the arcsinh-Cauchy prediction. This is because the Logit-Logistic distribution will satisfy the prediction-accuracy requirement over a substantially greater range of uncertainty of the estimated parameters,  
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         , than either of the other two distributions. In short, we are more confident in the Logit-Logistic prediction because its robustness is substantially greater.

ε 
                          is the greatest acceptable prediction error, as stated in Eq.  (32) . How large a prediction error is acceptable depends on the specific application and is a judgment made by the analyst. Part of that judgment is based on the trade off between robustness and prediction accuracy: small  
                            ε 
                          (which is good) entails small robustness (which is bad). If the analyst is willing to accept a prediction error  
                            ε 
                          greater than the  
                            ε 
                         -value at which the curves cross, then the alternative parameters provide far greater robustness to uncertainty in the true values than is provided by the putative estimates. Robustness to parameter uncertainty is important because we are applying data from one population to another population.

We begin, in Section  6.1 , by briefly reviewing some statistical conceptions of robustness and their relationship to info-gap robustness. Sections  6.2 and 6.3  develop info-gap robustness to distributional uncertainty and Section  6.4  contains an example.

The concept of robustness has spawned a large literature in statistics, which has some overlap with but also important divergences from the info-gap concept of robustness. An in-depth exegesis on the relations between info-gap theory and statistical robustness is beyond the scope of this paper. Instead, we limit coverage here to a brief introductory treatment. A review of the history of robust statistical concepts and research is available  (Portnoy & He, 2000) , and  Wilcox (2005)  provides a comprehensive introduction to robust statistical techniques for estimation and hypothesis testing.

A popular criterion for robustness in the statistical sense is whether “small” perturbations in a data-set yield only “small” changes in parameter estimates and other relevant properties of a statistical model. Often a “small” change in the data is operationalized by substituting a new datum for one already in the data-set. The classic comparison between a robust and non-robust parameter is the median versus the mean. Replacing one datum with another will at most shift the median to an adjacent datum, if it affects the median at all. However, if the value that the datum can assume is unbounded, then this substitution can make the mean arbitrarily large or small. Note that this is not the case for a  
                            γ 
                         -trimmed mean (i.e., the mean of a data-set whose distribution has been truncated at the  
                            γ 
                          and  
                            1 
                            − 
                            γ 
                          quantiles, for  
                            0 
                            < 
                            γ 
                            < 
                            0 
                            . 
                            5 
                         ), because if the new datum’s quantile rank lies below  
                            γ 
                          or above  
                            1 
                            − 
                            γ 
                          it gets trimmed. So a  
                            γ 
                         -trimmed mean is more robust than the (untrimmed) mean.

Formalizing these ideas somewhat further, consider an  
                            α 
                         -contaminated cdf:  
                            
                               (50) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        W 
                                     
                                     
                                        α

                                     x

                            x

                            y

                            x

                            W

                               x

                          can be made arbitrarily close by sufficiently small  
                            α 
                         .

A standard approach to evaluating the influence on a parameter, say,  
                            θ 
                         , of having the value  
                            y 
                          occur with probability  
                            α 
                         , as in Eq.  (50) , is to evaluate:  
                            
                               (51) 
                               
                                  T 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     , 
                                     α 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              θ 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           y 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        − 
                                        
                                           
                                              θ 
                                           
                                           
                                              ̂ 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         where  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          is the estimate of the parameter  
                            θ 
                          from the original data-set and  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                          is the estimate under the  
                            α 
                         -contaminated data-set.  
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               , 
                               α 
                               ) 
                            
                          is known as an “influence function”. A parameter  
                            θ 
                          is said to have “infinitesimal” robustness if  
                            
                               
                                  lim 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                                  → 
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               , 
                               α 
                               ) 
                            
                          is bounded, where the limit is taken as  
                            α 
                          approaches 0 from above. Another indicator of robustness is the “breakdown point”, which is the smallest value of  
                            α 
                          for which  
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               , 
                               α 
                               ) 
                            
                          can go to positive or negative infinity. Because for any  
                            α 
                            > 
                            0 
                         , a datum from an unbounded variable can shift the mean to positive or negative infinity, the breakdown point for the mean is 0. For the median, by contrast, the breakdown point is the maximum possible,  
                            α 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            5 
                         . A breakdown point cannot exceed 0.5 because if half of the observations are contaminated, it is not possible to distinguish between the underlying distribution and the contaminating distribution.

Roughly speaking, the breakdown point is akin to the info-gap concept of robustness. The breakdown point is the largest  
                            α 
                          that does not cause the influence function to go to positive or negative infinity. We can express this requirement as:  
                            
                               (52) 
                               
                                  T 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     , 
                                     α 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              θ 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           y 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        − 
                                        
                                           
                                              θ 
                                           
                                           
                                              ̂ 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                  
                                  < 
                                  ∞ 
                               
                            
                         If this relation is satisfied for large  
                            α 
                         , then the original estimator,  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         , is robust in the sense that its deviation from the contaminated estimator,  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                         , is bounded even for large contamination.

In Section  6.2  we will introduce info-gap models for distributional uncertainty that extend the  
                            α 
                         -contamination model of Eq.  (50)  by including explicit uncertainty in the shape of the contaminating distribution as well as its statistical weight. In Section  6.3  we will explore the properties of info-gap robustness for these info-gap models of distributional uncertainty. Section  6.4  presents an example.

The emphasis of this info-gap model is different from the emphasis of the  
                            α 
                         -contamination model of Eq.  (50) .  
                            U 
                            
                               ( 
                               h 
                               , 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                          focuses on distributional uncertainty, while  
                            α 
                         -contamination focuses on robustness to single-datum alteration of the data.

                            α 
                         , that does not cause the estimation error (as assessed by the influence function) to increase without bound. The robustness,
