This example uses data from the “Weather task” in the study reported by  Smithson and Segale (2009) , in which participants were asked to judge how likely Sunday is to be the hottest day of next week. Replicating a two-condition experiment by  Fox and Rottenstreich (2003) , participants were randomly assigned to a Case Prime condition (see below) which invokes a two-fold partition by focusing on whether Sunday will or will not be hottest, or a Class Prime condition (see below) which invokes a seven-fold partition by focusing on the hottest day of the week. This task has a “correct” partition, namely the seven-fold. The main hypothesis was that the tendency to provide probabilities anchored around 1/2 would be more pronounced in the Case than in the Class condition. The primes are as follows:

Case Prime

• 
                                  
                               
                               “What is the probability that the temperature at Canberra airport on Sunday will be higher than every other day next week?” 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     • 
                                  
                               
                               “What is the probability that the temperature at Canberra airport on Sunday will not be higher than every other day next week?”

“What is the probability that the temperature at Canberra airport on Sunday will be higher than every other day next week?”

“What is the probability that the temperature at Canberra airport on Sunday will not be higher than every other day next week?”

Class Prime

• 
                                  
                               
                               “What is the probability that the highest temperature of the week at Canberra airport will occur on Sunday?” 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     • 
                                  
                               
                               “What is the probability that the highest temperature of the week at Canberra airport will not occur on Sunday?”

“What is the probability that the highest temperature of the week at Canberra airport will occur on Sunday?”

“What is the probability that the highest temperature of the week at Canberra airport will not occur on Sunday?”

Thus, participants were asked to provide estimates of the probability that Sunday would be the hottest day (the “yes” judgments), and of the probability that it would not be the hottest (the “no” judgments). The data from 171 participants are included in the analyses presented here. To simplify analyses, the “no” probabilities have been subtracted from 1 (i.e., reversed), thereby enabling them to be compared directly with the “yes” probabilities.

The models presented here are based on ahypothesis that the probability estimates are a mixture of a uniform distribution (estimates from people who are completely unsure about the probability of the event) and a CDF-Quantile distribution that is not uniform (estimates from people who have definite views about the probability of the event). For any CDF-Quantile distribution we define the mixture model as:

                                  α

                                     x

                            G

We compare two such mixture models, which specify the distributions

                            μ

                            G

                            μ

The info-gap model under consideration is a direct application of Eq.  (55) :

                            α

                            Γ

The predicted probability is  
                            P

                            ε 
                         . That is, the prediction is considered reliable if it errs no more than  
                            ε 
                         . A large value of robustness,  
                            
                               
                                  h

                            Γ

                            α

                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          implies vulnerability to error and unreliability of the prediction. Thus the robustness function is the greatest horizon of uncertainty,  
                            h 
                         , up to which the prediction is reliable, which is the same as Eq.  (67)  for the special case of  
                            
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         :  
                            
                               
                                  (77)

In  Fig. 6  we plot the robustness functions for  
                            γ 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            5 
                         , 0.75, and 0.95, based on evaluating the inverse robustnesses with  Proposition 11 . We now discuss the significance of these results.

Recall that the logit-logistic distribution has far greater log-likelihood than the T2-Cauchy distribution, when using the putative mixture model, namely with  
                            
                               
                                  α 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  Γ 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         . However, the putative preference for logit-logistic over T2-Cauchy is uncertain (or even dubious) in light of the fact that the robustness of a predicted quantile rank is zero. That is, we see in each frame of  Fig. 6  that  
                            
                               
                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            0 
                          when  
                            ε 
                            = 
                            0 
                         : relying on the prediction of each model has no robustness to uncertainty in  
                            α 
                          and  
                            Γ 
                         . This is the “zeroing” property characteristic of all info-gap robustness functions. It quantifies the intuition that predictions based on uncertain evidence are themselves uncertain.

Moreover, the T2-Cauchy model is slightly more robust than the logit-logistic model throughout part of the relevant  
                            ε 
                         -range, but a dramatic reversal in robust preference occurs at larger values of  
                            ε 
                         , as we now explain.

The positive slopes of the robustness curves in  Fig. 6  imply that positive robustness is obtained only by allowing positive prediction error:  
                            
                               
                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                          increases (which is good) only by allowing  
                            ε 
                          to increase (which is not good). This expresses the irrevocable trade off between robustness and outcome-requirement: stricter requirement (smaller  
                            ε 
                         ) entails lower robustness to uncertainty (smaller  
                            
                               
                                  h 
                               
                               
                                  ̂ 
                               
                            
                         ). The slope of a robustness curve quantifies the “cost” of robustness, which varies along the curve: steep slope implies that a small increase of  
                            ε 
                          (the cost) results in large increase in robustness (the benefit).

In the left frame of  Fig. 6  we see that the T2-Cauchy mixture has slightly lower cost of robustness than the logit-logistic mixture, causing the T2-Cauchy robustness to be slightly greater. For instance, at  
                            ε 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            2 
                          the T2-Cauchy robustness is 0.205 while the logit-logistic robustness is only 0.156. This robustness-preference for T2-Cauchy is repeated in the middle and right frames as well, albeit slightly less pronounced. Even though the logit-logistic mixture is putatively a better fit to the data (its log-likelihood far exceeds that of the T2-Cauchy mixture), the logit-logistic robustness to non-probabilistic uncertainty (in the value of  
                            α 
                          and the shape of  
                            Γ 
                         ) is slightly less. Recalling, from the zeroing property, that the predicted quantile ranks have zero robustness, one might well prefer the putatively sub-optimal mixture, T2-Cauchy, due to its greater robustness.

All three robustness curves reach infinity at specific values of  
                            ε 
                         , as predicted by Eq.  (72)  of  Proposition 11 . This asymptote occurs at different values of  
                            ε 
                          for T2-Cauchy and logit-logistic in all frames of  Fig. 6 , causing the robustness curves to cross one another. Thus in the left-hand panel the logit-logistic mixture has unbounded robustness at  
                            ε 
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            65 
                          while the T2-Cauchy mixture has finite robustness at this value of  
                            ε 
                         . If prediction error of 0.65 in the quantile rank (which is rather large) is acceptable, then the logit-logistic mixture is more robust and hence preferred over the T2-Cauchy mixture at this value of  
                            ε 
                         . In other words, T2-Cauchy is more robust than logit-logistic at low  
                            ε 
                         , and hence T2-Cauchy is preferred at low  
                            ε 
                         , while logit-logistic is more robust and hence preferred at large  
                            ε 
                         . The crossing of the robustness curves entails the possibility for reversal of preference between these modeling options. As discussed in Section  5.4 , the analyst’s choice of the greatest acceptable prediction error,  
                            ε 
                         , depends on the specific application, and is also influenced by the trade off between robustness and prediction error.

Probability distributions characterize populations and support assessment and prediction. The CDF-Quantile distribution family is an example of collections of distributions that can provide versatility in modeling and prediction useful for capturing the subtleties of population variability. However, as is well known in psychological modeling and other areas, even populations that are putatively identical or similar may differ in significant but highly uncertain ways, making prediction difficult.

This paper has explored info-gap robustness analysis in conjunction with CDF-Quantile models. We have studied two types of non-probabilistic Knightian uncertainty: parameter uncertainty and distributional uncertainty. While both sources of uncertainty also are addressed in the robust statistics literature, the framework presented here goes beyond robust statistics in both the scope and nature of the uncertainties being addressed. In particular, we have demonstrated methods for assessing the robustness to uncertainty in the shape of the CDF’s, not only in their parameters or relative weights. In addition, we address the problem of generalizing from one population to another similar population whose degree of difference is uncertain.

Section  5  focuses on uncertainty in the location and shape parameters,  
                         μ 
                       and  
                         σ 
                      , of CDF-Quantile distributions. Statistically optimal estimates of these parameters are available based on measurements of a population in specific circumstances. However, we do not know the magnitude of difference between these estimates and valid parameter values for a different population (or the same population in different circumstances). The analyst’s goal is to predict a quantile rank in the un-measured population with specified accuracy. The info-gap robustness function answers the question: how wrong can the parameter estimates be, and the prediction is still adequately accurate? When the robustness is large, the prediction is reliable because it is immune to large estimation error. Conversely, small robustness implies vulnerability to estimation error and low prediction reliability.

Furthermore, the robustness function is evaluated for alternative estimators, thus enabling prioritization of these estimators in terms of their robustness to Knightian uncertainty. We have shown, by example, that putatively optimal estimators may be less robust than sub-optimal estimators, suggesting that one might prefer a sub-optimal estimator. The point is that the putatively optimal estimator  is  optimal vis-à-vis the stochastic variability of the data, but not vis-à-vis the Knightian uncertainty. In fact, these two attributes – statistical accuracy and info-gap robustness – trade off against one another. The analyst specifies acceptable statistical accuracy of prediction, and the info-gap robustness assesses the corresponding immunity to Knightian uncertainty. The robustness function enables the analyst to choose an acceptable trade off (if there is one) and to prioritize estimators accordingly. Generic propositions specify the robustness functions and their quantitative trade off, and characterize a class of situations in which preference for sub-optimal estimators can occur.

The irrevocable trade off between statistical fidelity to data and robustness to Knightian uncertainty –asserted in  Proposition 1  and illustrated in the examples –bears on the perennial problem of overfitting. Overfitting is the phenomenon of adapting a model to capture both the generic trend of the data as well as the noise in the data. High fidelity to data does not necessarily indicate high fidelity to the underlying trend because the fidelity may be to the noise. The trade off between fidelity and robustness touches on the overfitting challenge because overfitting typically occurs when the model has too many parameters. If small changes in the model would greatly erode the fidelity –implying low robustness – then overfitting may be indicated. Conversely, large robustness implies that fidelity is relatively immune to changes in model structure or parameters, suggesting that overfitting is avoided. Thus the trade off between fidelity and robustness may provide insight to the overfitting problem, which is worthy of further study. Further exploration could also involve robustness of the fidelity to uncertainty in the data itself, explored elsewhere ( Pierce et al., 2006 ;  Zacksenhouse et al., 2009 ).

Section  6  studies uncertainty in the weight and distributional shape of a contaminating sub-population. A CDF-Quantile distribution and a hypothesized sub-population are estimated based on data from a given population. However, both the weight and functional form of the sub-population distribution are uncertain. As before, the analyst wishes to predict a quantile rank of a different population with specified accuracy. The info-gap robustness evaluates the greatest error in the estimated weight and shape of the contamination, up to which the prediction is adequately accurate. Once again, alternative estimators can be prioritized according to their robustness to Knightian uncertainty. We show that the putatively optimal estimator is not always the most robust to uncertainty.

In recent commentaries on the so-called replication crisis in psychology, some scholars (e.g.,  Greenfield, 2017 ) have pointed out that several psychological domains are influenced by shifts in economic, cultural, and social factors so that studies in these domains conducted now or in the past are unlikely to be replicable in the future. Examples of such historically-malleable and culturally-sensitive domains include social and political psychology, as might be expected, but also less obvious domains such as developmental psychology. How big a shift may the outcomes of a study undergo before the study’s findings should be regarded as outdated or irrelevant? Are some types of results, estimates, or experimental effects more robust than others? Indeed, do some theories stand on a more robust corpus of evidence than their rivals? None of these questions can be addressed without methods for comparative assessments of robustness to Knightian uncertainties. It is thus useful and productive to combine statistical modeling, estimation, and forecasting with the management of non-probabilistic uncertainty using the tools illustrated in this paper. Furthermore, this paper focused on maximum likelihood estimation for predicting quantile rank. The methodology developed here can be applied to other estimators for predicting other quantities. One could also use the methodology for comparing conceptually distinct estimators.

                      . High robustness implies that the estimate is reliable. Low robustness implies that even small deviation in  
                         μ 
                       and  
                         σ 
                       can cause estimation error greater than  
                         ν 
                      , and that this estimate is not reliable.

Proof of Proposition 12 
                         Let  
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  h 
                                  , 
                                  γ 
                                  , 
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             denote the inner maximum in the definition of the robustness in Eq.  (116) . We will show that Eqs.  (118)  and  (119)  specify this inner maximum. 
                         
                            1. Sub-robustness functions.  For specified quantile rank  
                               γ 
                            , the corresponding quantile for the CDF-quantile distribution with parameters  
                               μ 
                             and  
                               σ 
                             is obtained from Eq.  (25) :  
                               
                                  (120) 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           γ 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        μ 
                                        , 
                                        σ 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     H 
                                     
                                        
                                           σ 
                                           
                                              
                                                 F 
                                              
                                              
                                                 − 
                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              γ 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           + 
                                           μ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         From Eq.  (120)  we see that, if  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  γ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ≥ 
                               0 
                            , then  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     γ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is maximal at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                             when  
                               μ 
                             and  
                               σ 
                             are maximal, and  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     γ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is minimal at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                             when  
                               μ 
                             and  
                               σ 
                             are minimal. 
                         Similarly, from Eq.  (120)  we see that, if  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  γ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               < 
                               0 
                            , then  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     γ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is maximal at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                             when  
                               μ 
                             is maximal and  
                               σ 
                             is minimal, and  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     γ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is minimal at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                             when  
                               μ 
                             is minimal and  
                               σ 
                             is maximal. 
                         In short, we see that  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     γ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  σ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             takes its extreme values, at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                            , when  
                               μ 
                             and  
                               σ 
                             take one of the four values,  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                            , specified in Eqs.  (82) – (85)  in the proof of  Proposition 5 . Consequently, we see that  
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  h 
                                  , 
                                  γ 
                                  , 
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             takes its maximum value, at horizon of uncertainty  
                               h 
                            , at one of these four values of  
                               
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                            . 
                         Thus the robustness, at specified  
                               ν 
                            , equals one of the following four sub-robustness functions:  
                               
                                  (121)

                                                 , 
                                                 γ

                                                 q 
                                                 , 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       r 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       a 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                              = 
                                              max 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    h 
                                                    : 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       | 
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             x 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             γ 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          ( 
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                r 
                                                             
                                                             
                                                                i 
                                                                j 
                                                             
                                                          
                                                          ) 
                                                       
                                                       − 
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             x 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             γ 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          ( 
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                μ 
                                                             
                                                             
                                                                a 
                                                             
                                                          
                                                          , 
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                σ 
                                                             
                                                             
                                                                a

                                                    ≤ 
                                                    ν 
                                                 
                                              
                                              
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                              
                                              for

                                              ∈ 
                                              
                                                 { 
                                                 1 
                                                 , 
                                                 2 
                                                 } 
                                              
                                              , 
                                              
                                              
                                              j

                                     , as stated following Eq.  (116) . This completes the proof of Eq.  (123) .
